description: Structured discovery questions for SOC 2 compliance readiness assessment. AI agents use these to interview the user and auto-create system/vendor/policy records from answers.
instructions: Ask questions in section order. For each answer, check creates_records to auto-create entities. Check policy_input to note which policy template this answer informs. Follow follow_up logic when conditions match.

questions.question: What is your company's legal name (as it would appear on contracts)?
What brand name do your customers know you by?
How many employees does your company have?
questions.context: This affects the scale and formality of controls needed.

questions.question: What industry are you in, and what does your product/service do?
questions.context: Some industries have additional regulatory requirements beyond SOC 2.

questions.question: Are you pursuing SOC 2 Type 1 (point-in-time snapshot) or Type 2 (proves controls work over 3-12 months)?

follow_up.question: Type 1 proves your controls exist at a point in time — faster but less valuable. Type 2 proves they work consistently over 3-12 months — what most customers require. I recommend starting with Type 2 preparation. Shall we proceed with Type 2?

questions.question: Which Trust Service Criteria are in scope? Security is always required. Do your customers also need assurance about Availability, Confidentiality, Privacy, or Processing Integrity?
questions.context: If unsure, start with Security only. You can add categories later.

questions.question: Do you have any existing compliance frameworks? (ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS, GDPR, etc.)
questions.context: Existing frameworks accelerate SOC 2 — many controls overlap.

questions.question: Which cloud providers does your company use? (AWS, GCP, Azure, DigitalOcean, etc.)

Do you have AWS access keys available for automated evidence collection? Our AWS collector checks IAM MFA, S3 encryption, RDS backups, security groups, CloudTrail, and 14 other services automatically.
What AWS region is your primary infrastructure in?
What databases do you use in production? (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB, etc.) For each, where is it hosted?
What are your main production applications/services? List each with a one-line description of what it does.
How is your network segmented? Do you use VPCs, subnets, security groups, or firewalls?
How do your employees log in to company systems? Do you use SSO, individual passwords, or something else?
Is multi-factor authentication (MFA) required? For everyone, or only certain roles/systems?
When a new employee starts, how do they get access to the systems they need? Who approves it? And when someone leaves, how quickly is their access revoked?
How often do you review who has access to what? Monthly? Quarterly? Never?
Is your data encrypted at rest? What method? (e.g., AES-256, AWS KMS managed keys)
Is data encrypted in transit? (TLS/SSL on all endpoints, VPN for internal traffic?)
What monitoring and alerting do you have? (CloudWatch, Datadog, PagerDuty, Grafana, etc.) What triggers an alert?
If a security incident happened right now — say someone reported a suspicious login — what would you actually do? Walk me through your real process, not what you'd ideally like to do.
Do you do vulnerability scanning or penetration testing? What tools? How often?
Do employees get security awareness training? What does it look like — formal course, onboarding briefing, nothing?
What source control platform do you use? (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, AWS CodeCommit)
What is your GitHub organization name?
Which repos should we monitor for compliance evidence? (comma-separated)
Are code reviews (pull requests) required before merging? Required or optional? How many reviewers?
What's your CI/CD setup? (GitHub Actions, Jenkins, CodePipeline, manual deploys?) Walk me through what happens from 'code merged' to 'running in production'.
Do you have separate dev, staging, and production environments?
What project management tool do you use to track work? (KanbanZone, Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues, Trello, etc.)
What is your KanbanZone board public ID? (Found in Board Settings > API)
How do you handle emergency hotfixes that need to bypass the normal process?
What uptime do you promise or target? (e.g., 99.9% SLA)
How are backups done? What's backed up, how often, and where are backups stored? Has a backup restore ever been tested?
If your primary data center or cloud region went down, what would happen? Do you have a disaster recovery plan? When was it last tested?
What's your Recovery Time Objective (how long until service is restored) and Recovery Point Objective (how much data can you afford to lose)?
Do you classify your data into sensitivity levels? What are they? (e.g., public, internal, confidential, restricted)
How long do you keep different types of data? When data is no longer needed, how do you dispose of it?
Do employees and contractors sign NDAs or confidentiality agreements?
What personal data do you collect from users or customers? (names, emails, payment info, usage data, IP addresses, etc.)
How do users consent to data collection? Do you have a privacy policy, cookie consent, terms of service?
If a user asked you to delete all their data, could you do it? How? How long would it take?
Are you subject to GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, PIPEDA, or other privacy regulations?
Does your data cross country borders? (e.g., users in EU, servers in US/Canada)
How do you validate input data in your applications? (form validation, API input sanitization, etc.)
How do you catch and handle errors? Do you have centralized error tracking? (Sentry, CloudWatch, etc.)
Do you have a QA or testing process before releases? Automated tests? Manual testing?
List all the SaaS tools and third-party services your company uses. For each, note the name, what it's used for, and whether it touches customer data.
Which of those vendors are sub-processors — meaning they process your customers' data on your behalf? (e.g., a payment processor, an email service that sends to your users)
When you pick a new vendor, what do you check? Do you look at their SOC 2 report, security page, privacy policy? Or do you just sign up?
